title: Unit 4 Checkpoint Quiz — Preterite Tense: Regular Verbs

questions.prompt: What is the yo preterite of 'hablar'?
questions.explanation: The yo preterite of -AR verbs ends in -é (with accent mark): hablé.

questions.prompt: What is the tú preterite of 'comer'?
questions.explanation: The tú preterite of -ER verbs ends in -iste: comiste.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the preterite?
questions.explanation: 'Ayer' signals a completed past action. 'Trabajé' is the correct yo preterite of trabajar.

questions.prompt: What spelling change does 'buscar' make in the yo preterite?
questions.explanation: Before 'e', c changes to qu to preserve the hard /k/ sound. So yo busqué, not yo buscé.

questions.prompt: For -ER and -IR verbs, the preterite endings are:
questions.explanation: -ER and -IR verbs share the same preterite endings: -í, -iste, -ió, -imos, -ieron. This makes them easier to learn together.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'They learned Spanish last year'?
questions.explanation: Ellos preterite of -ER verbs ends in -ieron: aprendieron. Time expression: el año pasado.

questions.prompt: Which time expression goes best with the preterite?
questions.explanation: 'Hace tres días' (three days ago) signals a specific, completed past action — perfect for the preterite. The others suggest habitual or ongoing actions.

questions.prompt: In Mexican storytelling, 'Fíjate que...' is used to:
options: Signal that a story or surprising event is about to be told
questions.explanation: 'Fíjate que' is a Mexican storytelling opener meaning 'So listen...' or 'You won't believe...' — it tells your listener to pay attention because something interesting is coming.
